education.notes: B.S. in chemical engineering; Ph.D. in industrial engineering completed 1950.

career.notes: First academic posting after MIT Ph.D.

career.organization: Case Institute of Technology (later Case Western Reserve)

career.role: Professor of Industrial Engineering, then founding chair Department of Computer Science
career.notes: Joined industrial engineering 1960; proposed and chaired the new Department of Computer Science from 1971; director of the Artificial Intelligence Laboratory; also appointments in linguistics and psychology in Weinberg College. Retired 2000.

narrative_blocks.header: He founded Northwestern's computer science department
narrative_blocks.body: Krulee proposed and in 1971 founded the Department of Computer Science at Northwestern University, becoming its first chair and hiring its founding faculty. He also directed the university's Artificial Intelligence Laboratory.

narrative_blocks.header: He was there at AI's first conference
narrative_blocks.body: Krulee attended the first International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence in 1969, near the field's very beginning. His later book examined how computers might process natural language.

narrative_blocks.header: He trained across chemistry, psychology, and engineering
narrative_blocks.body: Krulee took an MIT degree in chemical engineering, a master's in psychology, and an MIT doctorate in industrial engineering before settling into academic life. He taught at Michigan, Tufts, and Case before joining Northwestern in 1960.

narrative_blocks.header: He served as a Navy engineer in wartime
narrative_blocks.body: During the Second World War, Krulee was a Navy engineering officer from 1943 to 1946. In civilian life he kept up his trombone in a community ensemble well into old age.

sources.short: Northwestern McCormick obituary Jan 26 2017; Daily Northwestern Jan 26 2017

sources.short: Northwestern McCormick obituary Jan 26 2017; Daily Northwestern Jan 26 2017

career.role: Secretary, then administrator and program founder
career.organization: Northwestern University McCormick School of Engineering
career.notes: From early 1960s as Associate Dean William Brazelton's secretary; founded Women in Tech, helped create Northwestern SWE chapter (1976), launched Career Day for Girls (1971). Retired 1996. Profiled with successor Ellen Worsdall in McCormick Magazine Spring 2020.

narrative_blocks.header: Launched Career Day for Girls at Northwestern, 1971
narrative_blocks.body: Working as Associate Dean Brazelton's secretary at McCormick in the early 1960s, Carolyn found the six women enrolled in engineering studying alone — she convened them into a single class so they had partners. In 1971 she organized the first Career Day for Girls, drawing twenty grade-school students; by 2020 the program drew 139.

death.cause: Sudden death age 59 per published obituary.

parents.father_research_notes: Louis Charles Harms — non-Hooker. Eric's father per published obituary.
parents.mother_research_notes: Carol Harms, née Durband — non-Hooker. Eric's mother per published obituary.

INST044.name: Case Western Reserve University
INST044.notes: Founded 1826 in Hudson, Summit County, Ohio as Western Reserve College by a group of Connecticut Congregationalist settlers, with Rev. Giles Hooker Cowles (H00443) among its founders. Called 'the Yale of the West' — same motto (Lux et Veritas), same entrance standards, same curriculum as Yale. First college in northern Ohio. H00443 (Giles Hooker Cowles, gen 5 Hooker descendant) was named 'Hooker' for both his grandfather Giles Hooker and the Hooker ancestral line. He graduated Yale 1789, moved to Austinburg, Ashtabula County OH as a missionary (Ohio Home Missionary Society), and was instrumental in establishing the college infrastructure of the Western Reserve. First college west of the Appalachians to enroll (1832) and graduate (1836) an African-American student. Hudson was a major Underground Railroad stop. Co-founder Owen Brown was the father of abolitionist John Brown. The Newton family (HD2925 John Newton, HD2927 Eben Newton) and Elisha Whittlesey (X01997) all operated in the Western Reserve contemporaneously. Whittlesey was public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve from 1806; Eben Newton was a judge and state senator. Moved to Cleveland 1882, merged with Case Institute of Technology 1967. H00443 is the brother of H00442 (Pamelia Cowles Thompson), whose granddaughter Laura Hooker Thompson Newton (HD0532) married John Newton (HD2925) — making the founding of Western Reserve College a family affair in the deepest sense. Among the original 12-member founding board of three presbyteries that met at Warren OH 1825 to choose a college site (selecting Hudson, now Summit County): from Grand River Presbytery, Rev. Dr. Giles Hooker Cowles (H00443) and Rev. Eliphalet Austin (X01418, with Harvey Coe and A. Griswold); from Portage Presbytery, Rev. Joseph Treat, John Steward, J. H. Whittlesey, Lemuel Porter; from Huron Presbytery, A. H. Betts, L. B. Sullivan, Hon. Samuel Cowles (of San Francisco — his namesake nephew HD3710 was 2 years old in 1825), D. Betts. Charter obtained 1826.

INST124.name: Northwestern University
INST124.notes: Private research university founded 1851 in Evanston Illinois; McCormick School of Engineering and Applied Science includes the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science.
